critique.summary: The article discusses the history and fate of GEM, an early graphical user interface developed by Digital Research for IBM PCs and Atari ST computers. It highlights GEM's initial advantages, legal challenges from Apple, competition with Microsoft Windows, and its eventual decline, while noting its survival on the Atari ST and later open source releases.
mainPoints: GEM was developed by Digital Research and released in 1985, predating Windows 1.0 by about nine months.
Apple threatened to sue Digital Research over GEM's similarities to the Apple Lisa interface, leading to legal settlements and changes that made GEM less elegant.
GEM struggled on IBM PCs due to lack of software, performance issues on early CPUs, and competition from Windows, which gained traction with Windows 3.0 in 1990.
GEM found more success on the Atari ST, where hardware capabilities and bundled software made it more viable.
Software piracy on the Atari ST and the rise of Windows PCs led to GEM's decline in the early 1990s.
GEM continued as a graphical runtime library for DOS applications like Ventura Publisher and was later released as open source by Caldera in 1999, but has not been actively developed since 2008.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides an accurate overview of GEM's development, legal challenges, and competition with Windows, consistent with historical records. The timeline of GEM's release, Apple's legal actions, and Windows' rise aligns with documented events. The discussion of GEM's relative success on the Atari ST and its decline due to piracy and competition is also supported by credible sources. The mention of Caldera releasing GEM as open source in 1999 and the existence of FreeGEM and OpenGEM projects is factual. However, the article is primarily a personal retrospective and lacks citations or multiple perspectives, which limits its journalistic rigor.
considerations: The article does not cite external sources or provide multiple viewpoints, which would strengthen its reliability.
It does not discuss in detail the technical differences between GEM and competing GUIs like Windows or Mac OS.
The impact of Digital Research's business decisions and Microsoft's OEM agreements on GEM's market penetration could be further elaborated.
The article does not explore the broader market context, such as user preferences or software ecosystem development, beyond piracy and hardware performance.
More information on the open source GEM projects' impact and legacy would provide a fuller picture.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the history and impact of GEM and Digital Research, with users sharing their experiences and opinions on GEM's technical limitations, legal challenges, and legacy. Several comments highlight GEM's architecture, its use on Atari ST, and the reasons for its decline compared to MS-DOS and other platforms.

comments.author: tombert
comments.text:
I've been on a bit of a rabbit hole with Digital Research in the last few days, specifically because I am utterly fascinated with Concurrent DOS.

I played with Concurrent DOS (and later MultiUser DOS) in PCem and I was utterly amazed. I hadn't realized that there was a preemptive multitasking operating system available to consumers as early as the mid 80's outside of AmigaOS.

I read the Wikipedia and I kind of understand why it didn't catch on, but man I kind of wish DRI (and Gary Kildall) was still around. I suspect if they were, they would have continued to make stuff that was at least interesting.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-informed perspective on Digital Research's innovations, highlighting Concurrent DOS's advanced features and expressing a nuanced understanding of why it didn't succeed commercially.

comments.author: cmrdporcupine
comments.text:
GEM on the PC was... ick... compared to on the Atari ST.

But the problem with GEM on the Atari ST is that in order to cram it into the 192KB ROM they ripped out some goodies like proportional font support which ended up being in an add-on called "GDOS" which was buggy, used up RAM, and most people didn't have it (it came with things like DTP software etc).

In general this was always the problem with the ST. The Tramiels shipped it early and cheap and awesome and I loved mine ... but then didn't pay enough attention to software updates until it was too late and the world had moved on. Jack Tramiel never really understood the value of a good software platform IMHO.

In the early 90s they seemed to learn the error of their ways, hired some talent, and released full multitasking re-entrant versions of TOS/GEM ... but too late.

GEM's architecture itself underneath actually was clearly built for a mulitasking architecture complete with message passing between applications (via AES application msg send / mailboxes) etc. It just came down to failure to iterate.

Also the article mentions DR "hiring some people from Xerox" but in fact fails to note that the actual original architect and author of GEM itself was hired from Xerox (Lee Lorenzen). He joined up with DR because he tried to pitch Xerox on porting their Star office concepts down to PC-class hardware and they didn't go for it. His pitch video can actually be seen here:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EMBGRZftS30

Lorzenen later left DR and created Ventura Publisher.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a detailed and accurate critique of GEM's implementation on Atari ST and the business decisions that limited its success, supported by historical context and technical details.

comments.author: car
comments.text:
Apple sued DRI, which resulted in the crippling of GEM, the glaring one I remember were static windows. You heard that right, windows were not resizable but had fixed screen locations in the PC version.

Thankfully Atari licensed GEM for their 68000 machines before the lawsuit, and wasn't affected by these changes. The Atari ST (Sixteen/Thirtytwo) was very Mac like at the time. It even ran the Mac OS from Apple ROMs (Spectre 128 and Aladin) on its much cheaper hardware.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly references the Apple lawsuit's impact on GEM and provides a specific example of the limitations imposed, reflecting a knowledgeable understanding of GEM's history.

comments.author: zabzonk
comments.text:
> But the bigger problem was software piracy. Piracy was common on the ST, and that made developers less enthusiastic to continue ST development

Not so sure about this. The Atari/GEM combination was very popular with musicians for MIDI, and I don't think there was so much piracy, or at least not compared with other platforms of the time.

The reasons I didn't develop anything much for Gem - a) It was quite difficult b) Not well documented c) I was too busy playing Dungeon Master.

I think many others may have similar thoughts.
comments.analysis: This comment challenges a common narrative about piracy affecting GEM development, offering a plausible alternative explanation based on personal experience and the platform's niche popularity.

comments.author: tengwar2
comments.text: I can't say I'm wild about a world where Digital Research won. When they were dominant with CP/M, the tools and documentation were bad to the point where most machines had Z80 processors and DR only provided an 8080 assembler, so you had to DB significant bits of code to get the missing opcodes. Developing RSXs to access bank-switched memory under CP/M 3 could have been so much easier with a few examples and perhaps debugging tools. MS/DOS was just so much easier.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a critical but accurate perspective on Digital Research's early tooling and documentation shortcomings, explaining why MS-DOS was easier to develop for.

comments.author: chasil
comments.text:
I only used GEM in the form of Xerox Ventura Publisher. I used it to write all my undergrad papers.

It ran quite well on my 286.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ventura_Publisher
comments.analysis: This personal anecdote adds a user perspective on GEM's practical application, though it is less analytical and more experiential compared to other comments.

comments.author: ayaros
comments.text: There's now a version of GEM you can run on a Lisa. But 68KMLA is down so I have no easy way to link to the relevant thread. :(
comments.analysis: This brief comment provides a current update about GEM's availability on another platform, though it lacks depth or supporting detail.
